data.title: Seeking 15%+ Yields? Here Are 2 BDCs I'm Buying
data.description:
My closest followers have most likely noticed that when it comes to BDCs, I have become more bullish than earlier in the year before the sector entered into a free-fall.

However, as I laid it out in my recent piece on the BDC environment - The BDC Bargain Bell Is Ringing - there are still some unpleasant headwinds remaining such as lower base rates, tight base dividend coverage levels and generally exhausted balance sheets.

To navigate these issues and, importantly, to capitalize on the tailwinds from the increased M&A activity, I think that investors have to be very selective here. Namely, high-quality equity investment exposures, strong balance sheets with ample debt capacities and robust underwriting standards are key for success.

Having said that, while this is my overarching core strategy through which I expect to capture durable current income streams (without the expense of NAV loss), I have remained open to venturing in some tactical plays.

By a tactical play I mean BDCs, which offer very compelling price to value ratio. The value might be much lower (NAV of a lower quality) than for my core holdings, but as long as the price is even lower (deeply below NAV), I would for sure consider such investments in my portfolio. Granted, the allocation to them would be much lower than for core holdings; just to balance the risks and avoid potential income volatility.

In the article below, I would like to elaborate on two tactical BDCs, which I have included in my portfolio even though they lack the key characteristics that I typically seek in BDCs for sustainable income extraction.

Pick #1 - FS KKR Capital

The first one is FS KKR Capital (FSK). Here I have to provide a bit of a history.

My first article on FSK was issued in December 2023, where I compared FSK (the second-largest BDC) with Ares Capital (ARCC) (the largest BDC). I decided to go with FSK mainly because of much steeper discount - at that time around 20%. After a year of holding period, in January, 2025, I decided to harvest my profits, locking in ~27% in total returns. From that moment until I issued my recent piece on FSK, the BDC contracted by almost 30%.

Now, if we measured the performance of my FSK holding now, we would notice a positive result of ~8.8%, which is significantly above the BDC (BIZD) index.

I think that it would be fair to say that my FSK trades from the timing perspective have been quite acceptable.

The question is whether it is perhaps a good moment to quickly lock in the high single digit return and reallocate the capital into some other, higher quality BDCs.

Well, I do not think so. Here is why.

FSK continues to trade at a P/NAV of 0.70x, which indicates a discount of 30%. It is a huge number and also a meaningful gap from the sector average discount of 15%. What this implies is that the market is not buying FSK's NAV figure. In other words, the expectation is that going forward it will drop by converging closer to the current price. So, let's unpack the NAV.

In recent Q3, 2025 figures, FSK reported an increase in NAV per share by 2.7% on quarter-to-quarter basis. The reason for that was simple - net unrealized and realized gain component, which benefited from FSK's non-accrual restructuring and two profitable investment exits. Plus, there was another positive restructuring event right after Q3, which resulted in a further reduction in non-accrual rate and, of course, in an uptick of NAV per share. The non-accrual rate has declined to 1.9% of a FV basis (considering the post Q3 restructured loan). In this context, we have to also appreciate the fact that in Q3 FSK distributed $0.70 per share of dividend of which $0.06 per share was a supplemental. Based on the current share price the $0.70 per share payout translates to ~4.4% yield (in one quarter alone). So, even with this kind of aggressive payout, FSK managed to boost its NAV.

Speaking of non-accruals, I think that it would be fair to say that exactly this part of FSK's business is what worries the market most. In other words, the weak non-accrual history creates concerns about unfavorable surprises in the future. I have three things to say here:

  1. The past couple of quarters have clearly indicated that the trend in non-accruals is improved. There have still been some spikes (such as in Q2, 2025), the overarching direction of travel remains positive. This is logical considering the fact that most of the non-accruals over the past years have really stemmed from the Corporate Capital Trust side, which was merged in FSK back in 2019. We are now in the very late innings of a completely recycled loan portfolio that is underpinned by FSK underwritten loans.
  2. The underlying portfolio investment companies are in good shape as, for example, in Q3 their weighted average EBITDA generation increased by 4% on a year-over-year basis.
  3. The fact that base rates are lower and will likely continue to fall, FSK portfolio cash flows should benefit.

What could drive down NAV is the aggressive distributions. However, it has been already communicated by the management that going forward the base dividend will be at $0.45 per share and supported by complementary distributions in order to extract full NII per share out of the system. For example, for Q1, 2026, the expectation is to pay out $0.55 per share, which based on current share price leads to just below 15% yield. Plus, if we assume that Q3 NII per share remains in place at $0.57, the dividend story looks pretty strong.

Now, do I believe that FSK will manage to keep NII per share of $0.57? No. It will most likely go down with further base rate reductions and unfavorable spread convergence (i.e., current portfolio high spread investments being slowly but surely replaced with investments that carry market-level spreads).

Do I believe that FSK's NII per share will drop below $0.45 (by more than 22%)? Absolutely not. Even if we assume a very pessimistic scenario of $0.45 per share distributions, we would still be getting an enticing yield of ~11.5%

In my view, FSK is relatively stable NAV and high yield play with significant price upside due to abnormal discount.

Pick #2 - CION Investment

CION Investment Corporation (CION) is my second tactical BDC investment, which currently yields 14.9% and trades at a P/NAV of 0.66x (i.e., a discount of 34%). I have been long CION since August, 2024, where the trade has resulted in flat total returns. However, in the past couple of months, the share price has not only stabilized, but also shown some first signs of recovery. In last month specifically - which includes Q3 report - CION is up by ~8%, which is definitely one of the best performance in the sector.

Again, just as in the case of FSK, a 30%+ discount means that the market does not think that the current NAV figure is reflective of long-term value.

Obviously, CION has above average risk, no questions about that. But, in my view, the distance between price and value is too long. Namely, I am not arguing that CION should trade at NAV, but at least significantly closer to it than what the share price indicates now. I will explain.

The key driver for CION's discount is considerable payment-in-kind streams. The PIK explains the lion's share of total income, whereas the average level for BDCs is around 10%. The problem with PIK is that the income is non-cash and instead comes in the form of increased outstanding loan principal (investment value for CION). Usually, such structures are offered to struggling businesses to manage their cash flow issues. So, I fully understand why the market has assigned notable valuation discount to account for the potential losses (i.e., non-accruals).

However, if we look into the details, we will not see as bad picture as it might seem from the surface.

First of all, CION's non-accrual rate remains well below sector average. It is not in the first quartile, but at least it is not above sector average, where, as I said earlier, the average discount is 15% (not 34% as for CION).

Similarly, most of CION's loans are first-lien, which is the safest place to be from the BDC's perspective. More importantly, we have to consider this:

  • 2/3 of PIK loans are structured and not amended. It just confirms that going big on PIK is CION's inherent strategy, and not a byproduct of bad investments. For example, the spread that CION has for its direct strategy investments is 7% over SOFR and for opportunistic cases 14% over SOFR. For a typical core middle market BDC the spread over SOFR is currently around 4.5%. So, through PIK structures CION can both avoid spread compression headwinds and capture outsized yields.
  • About 67% of CION's PIK allocations are risk-rated either 1 or 2 and 98% risk-rated 3 or better. Anything that is below risk-rating 3 (i.e., 4 and 5) is already an indicate of underperforming investments. I think that you will agree with me that the balance here is very favorable.

What was really encouraging to see from Q3 data points was the fact that the NAV per share surged higher. The NAV increased from $14.50 per share in Q2 to $14.86 in Q3. The increase was driven by accretive share repurchases, unrealized appreciation and, importantly, realized (monetized) PIK investments, which drove the NII per share from $0.32 in Q2 to $0.74 in Q3. Had the PIK monetization not happened, the NII per share would have landed at the same level as in the prior quarter, which would support ~13% dividend yield (excluding NAV-funded component).

All of this is a clear proof that the market's fears about CION's NAV sustainability are overblown.

In my humble opinion, it will be a gradual process for CION's share price to converge closer to the NAV. It will take additional quarters like Q3 to convince the market. In the meantime, we can enjoy a compelling yield of almost 15%.

data.title: One Of The Best High-Yield Setups I've Ever Seen
data.description:
A.I. has been a dominant investing theme for the past several years, ever since ChatGPT came out and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) delivered very strong growth guidance. The boom has not only been confined to NVDA, either, as leading hyperscalers have soared and Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) has seen its stock price jettison higher on incredible market optimism about the future of A.I. to transform the global economy.

A.I. is turning the common maxim, "knowledge is power," on its head, as A.I. is now making it so that "power is knowledge." This means that energy (XLE) is the critical enabler for A.I., and therefore the A.I. boom is not just a bullish tailwind for tech companies but is also likely to be a major demand driver and potential enricher for energy infrastructure (UTF) and especially strategically located energy infrastructure companies.

Of course, as usual, Mr. Market, while often wrong, is not unintelligent and therefore has bid up many of these A.I. infrastructure names, including utility stocks (XLU) soaring higher anticipating dramatically increasing demand for electric power generation. Power producers like Vistra Corp. (VST) and Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG) have also generated very strong returns in recent years, and even recently, renewable power, iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (ICLN)-focused power producers like Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP), Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) and Clearway Energy, Inc. (CWEN)(CWEN.A). Even HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital, Inc. (HASI) has bumped meaningfully higher recently.

However, there still remain some major market disconnects in the energy infrastructure space with numerous significantly undervalued stocks that are positioned to still benefit some from the A.I. boom, and when combined with other significant strengths afforded by these investment opportunities, create one of the best high-yield investing setups I have ever seen. In this article, I will detail them.

Major Market Disconnects in Midstream Infrastructure

The area where I am seeing the most opportunity is in the midstream infrastructure space. As these companies, especially ones that have strategically located natural gas (FCG) infrastructure, are positioned to benefit from the A.I. boom, data centers will require a significant amount of clean, affordable, and dependable energy supply, which natural gas is positioned to perfectly benefit from. In fact, there are numerous midstream companies, including ONEOK, Inc. (OKE), Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI), TC Energy Corporation (TRP), Enbridge Inc. (ENB), and the The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB), along with many others that have reported significant opportunities to benefit from data center contracts that should be fairly capital light and deliver high returns on investment while also increasing the long-term economic value of their natural gas businesses in general. This tailwind extends even to businesses that are not positioned particularly close to where data centers are expected to be built because a rising tide lifts all boats, so growing demand for natural gas to power data centers will increase demand for natural gas across the energy value chain.

Why Midstream Still Offers Heads-I-Win, Tails-I-Don’t-Lose Much Potential

Not only do these midstream names stand to benefit from A.I., but they have many other strengths on their own right that make them heads, I win, tails, I do not lose much of anything, even if the A.I. boom fizzles out. This is because they have long-duration contracts, or even regulated assets, and they have long-duration contracts, or even regulated assets, or long-duration contracted or even regulated assets that make them generally commodity price resistant and even recession resistant in many cases.

Additionally, they have strong balance sheets with solid investment-grade credit ratings and leverage ratios that are as low as they have been in their entire histories in many cases. They also have solid growth outlooks, even beyond the A.I. data center tailwind, as they have other attractive opportunities to synergistically consolidate areas where they operate and or benefit from other growth drivers such as the LNG export boom or even persistent demand for crude (USO) and refined products (CRAK). Not only that, but they also have tax-advantaged distributions, especially if they are K-1 issuing MLPs (AMLP) as distributions tend to be classified as return of capital, and these companies, as pass-through entities, are also exempted from corporate income tax, making them highly efficient investment vehicles in a taxable account.

When you combine all that with the fact that many of them offer high single digit or even double-digit distribution yields, trade at relatively attractive EV to EBITDA multiples, and have mid to high single-digit EBITDA and distributed cash flow per unit CAGR outlooks for the next several years, these investments look like not only highly compelling and sustainable income machines but also total return compounders.

Global Energy Exports Offer Another Tailwind

Additionally, even these businesses' energy export assets should benefit from the A.I. boom, as countries in Asia and Europe will likely also need to significantly ramp up their energy imports to be able to power data centers and A.I. applications in their own countries, leading to the potential for LNG exports from the U.S. to these countries to help bridge the energy deficit. This means that investors have a golden opportunity to take advantage of the current market disconnect as investors seem to be currently focused on the role of utilities and power generators in the A.I. space but are not sufficiently valuing some midstream companies’ abilities to provide vital infrastructure for natural gas to power data centers and for energy exports in the U.S. to other markets around the world that will be increasingly energy-hungry to power their own A.I. ambitions. Some of my favorite overlooked opportunities include Energy Transfer LP (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), as well as the aforementioned OKE. All three of these have very attractive well-covered yields with promising long-term growth runways. They also have well-diversified business models that include access to the NGL export boom, as well as natural gas-related data center projects, and have strong investment-grade credit ratings.

Risks to Consider and Investor Takeaway

That being said, no investment is risk-free, and it is certainly possible that the A.I. build-out will fall well short of expectations, which could somewhat hurt the growth prospects for these midstream businesses. However, on a risk-adjusted basis, they will be hurt far less than the leading A.I. names in both the energy infrastructure space and in the tech stocks (XLK) that the market is currently obsessed with. Therefore, this is a much lower-risk way to still gain upside and significant passive income from the A.I. boom that will likely still turn out to be solid investments over the long term, even if it fails to pan out.

Additionally, near-term energy price weakness is weighing on midstream, which could persist if energy prices remain weak and especially if they weaken further. However, this is largely an overstated headwind for midstream as their cash flows are largely commodity price resistant. Therefore, the current pullback in the sector provides a great buying opportunity for patient investors as it increases the yield and the potential capital appreciation that can be obtained from these infrastructure investments over the long term.

Given the fact that the A.I. build-out and expected A.I. boom are driving strong energy demand in the United States, as well as expected strong demand for energy exports from the United States, the economic value of strategically located natural gas and NGL infrastructure appears to be quite high. Yet, the market has not seemed to want to price that in for some natural gas and NGL infrastructure players like OKE, ET, and EPD. As a result, investors have a great opportunity to take advantage of one of the best high-yield investing opportunities I have ever seen by loading up on these names. As a result, these, along with other midstream names, make up my largest position in my portfolio by far, including overweight positions in energy infrastructure in my Core and Retirement portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Disney: The Market Is Finally Waking Up To The Danger Of The YouTube TV Blackout
data.description:
After The Walt Disney Company (DIS) reported its full-year results for Fiscal Year 2025 yesterday, I at first expected the stock to trade choppily in a more or less sideways manner. What followed was instead a nearly 10% decline in the price of the stock, all the way down from $116 to $107. Analysts reactions, perhaps in part informed by the stock’s movements, have generally accentuated the negatives of the report.

What was interesting about this decline was that it wasn’t purely a reaction to the earnings report itself; the decline began in earnest after the subsequent conference call began. I believe management has made the wrong choice in terms of how it has chosen to frame and approach the ongoing blackout dispute with YouTube TV. It is this, not the earnings numbers themselves - which really weren’t that bad - that has sent the stock spiraling.

Be warned, we have several issues to cover, and I intend to go in-depth; I’m afraid this article will be a little longer than you’re used to. I hope you find it worth the extra few minutes.

The Report Itself Was Actually Solid

Before turning to the main issue, first, the report itself. It’s true that Disney did disclose a substantial decline in free cash flow in the fourth quarter, but I would argue it is a mistake to emphasize too much a number so inherently volatile on a quarter-to-quarter basis. Disney’s Parks, cruise liners, and other major CAPEX outlays can cause timing issues in FCF; the more stable annual FCF number was actually up 18% Y/Y.

Mind you, I wouldn’t put too much stock in that either; Disney also disclosed a number of one-off factors, including some tax allocation adjustments. I am not a tax lawyer and will not pretend to understand more than I do, but it seems reasonably clear from Disney’s own disclosures that the federal emergency declaration pertaining to the California wildfires has allowed the company to defer roughly $1.7 billion of taxes owed from this fiscal year to next. Strip that out, and FCF was more or less steady Y/Y. We could dive even deeper, but I would put that last number as far closer to the ‘true’ FCF than either of the previous two.

At the same time, Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act will also offer a permanent reduction in taxes owed of some size, owing to the expansion of full expensing - the rare tax topic I do know something about - to more qualified spending. I doubt that will fully attenuate the impact of the $1.7 billion on next year’s numbers, but it will help. Until we see how much it will help, however, I am reluctant to base a recommendation on such an unknown.

ESPN's Ongoing, Hidden Decline

Let’s move on, then, to the company’s actual operations. The report was somewhat occluded by the fact that most of the most interesting things happening at Disney didn’t start until after the report ended; ESPN didn’t terminate its betting partnership with PENN Entertainment (PENN) and launch its new one with DraftKings (DKNG) until this month. ESPN Unlimited did launch in August, but Disney offered no numbers on it.

In fact, although it was not remarked upon by the analysts on the call, that was, to me, one of the more alarming elements of the report. To be sure, management insists that ESPN Unlimited is off to a fantastic start; they’re “thrilled” with the response.

But the company is offering less transparency into sports streaming instead of more. For a long time, I had to approach my ESPN analysis indirectly, even abstractly, because Disney did not break out ESPN’s performance separately from ABC, Disney Channel, and the rest. That finally changed; in late 2023, Disney broke out ESPN’s financials separately for the first time. Employees, and more importantly, former employees, were stunned to discover that the division was still reporting multibillion-dollar profits. The backlash was actually considerable, as employees questioned why such drastic layoffs were needed if the division was doing so well.

For the record, they shouldn’t have been quite so upset as all that; ESPN is not doing nearly as well as is portrayed. As I’ve explained before, much of the ESPN “profit” is in fact reallocated revenues from the Disney+ streaming service, funneled through ESPN+. In FY2024, almost 70% of ‘Sports’ profits were in fact ESPN+ profits, and almost all of those were really Disney+/Hulu profits. ‘Core’ ESPN is almost certainly now generating less than $1 billion in operating income per year. My previous article covered this in depth, so I won’t repeat those points again, you can see all my calculations demonstrating this there.

These trends have continued in the past fiscal year. In Disney’s Q2 earnings report in March, "Sports" reported $428 million in subscription fees. No, that’s not a typo; ESPN linear generates far more than that, of course, but linear monthly fees are called "affiliate fees." The term "subscription fees" connotes payments to the ESPN+ streaming service. Those payments, in turn, are largely payments on the Disney Bundle, which are allocated internally to ESPN+ by Disney, not by arm's-length transactions.

That $428 million represented 63% of the $687 million operating income of the division. That is up from the 54% they represented the prior year. Not surprising, considering that ESPN+ subscription fees are (nominally, by Disney’s internal accounting) rising while operating income at the Sports division is falling.

The Q3 report was broadly similar. Thus, ESPN Core’s operating income is falling even faster, driven by two things. One of them, of course, is cord-cutting.

Ever-Foggier Reporting

With the launch of ESPN Unlimited, however, Disney has closed this rare peek behind the curtain of its operations. In the just released report, Disney reported only a combined "affiliate and subscription fees" category for its Sports division; this despite the fact that it continues to report the two categories separately in its Entertainment division.

I won’t speculate as to reasons or motives; the fact is that all we know for sure is that transparency has been reduced. However, this does make it somewhat harder to determine how the interaction between streaming and linear is playing out in Disney’s operations. And I cannot help but think that if there were good news to report here, Disney would not be so reluctant to continue reporting it.

The issue is not unique to Disney, of course; I have said in the past that the line between streaming and linear has become so blurred at most legacy media companies that it is impossible to tell if the operating incomes being reported for each category fairly reflect the economics of each. The only thing you can really do is look at the company’s operations as a whole.

Earnings Essentials

So, I’m going to take my own advice. Overall, Disney reported revenue growth of 3% - basically at par with U.S. inflation - but held cost growth to 1%. This year’s net income of $12.4 billion was a massive improvement on last year’s $5 billion, but as you’d expect, there were one-offs there; in addition to the factors already discussed, Disney also netted $2.8 billion in tax benefits from the acquisition of Hulu and did not repeat last year’s $3.5 billion restructuring and impairment charges related to Star India and domestic linear networks.

Experiences operating income was up while Sports operating income was down domestically; the sale of Star India ended the red ink in Disney’s international Sports operations.

Altogether, after one-offs, net income was very similar in both years, averaging about $8.7 billion. At first glance, that’s actually not so bad; at a market average 24 P/E multiple, that would pencil out almost exactly to the $116 per share it went into the report with. After yesterday’s decline, its TTM P/E is only 22.1.

Blackout Becomes A Knockout

Unfortunately, after the report comes the conference call, and it is here that I believe things started to go off the rails. Disney has been enmeshed for the last two weeks in a blackout dispute - yes, yet another one - this time with Google’s (GOOG, GOOGL) YouTube TV. The dispute has defied expectations that it would be resolved quickly; in fact, having passed Wednesday with no deal, it is now officially the longest blackout dispute in Disney’s history.

This is a blackout involving Disney’s core linear TV platform and one of the largest pay-TV operators in the U.S.; up until yesterday, the market had simply not treated it with the requisite intensity of focus. In fact, Disney’s stock was actually up 5% since the blackout began going into earnings, a frankly ludicrous appreciation that apparently signified the market’s confidence that even the mighty YouTube would soon come crawling back to ESPN, which management has always insisted is essential to the survival of pay-TV operators.

It hasn’t worked out like that. YouTube has held out. Even the broadcast of Monday Night Football’s most prominent game of the year, the Eagles-Packers matchup on Monday, did not break the impasse. As the early optimism has become harder to maintain, CEO Bob Iger has increasingly confined his media appearances to friendly platforms, usually ones he controls; and as expected, the national blackout has been the most obtrusive issue not raised by the interviewers. With the earnings call, there was no ducking it any longer.

To be clear, management knew this was coming; that’s why they reportedly kicked negotiations with YouTube - presumably already a high priority - into another gear, which saw Iger himself reach out to Google’s senior management. Reports were that substantial progress had been made; indeed, some even said Disney made more progress in 12 hours than it made in the two weeks before that. I would put that down to hyperbole, but the truth is that even such a marker probably doesn’t set the bar very high.

Despite the progress, full resolution eluded the parties; when the conference call rolled around, Disney was still blacked out. Iger and CFO Hugh Johnson got their questions, but the answers were not what the market hoped.

Pushing All The Chips In

In a nutshell, Disney isn’t compromising; it’s doubling down. Iger insists that Disney will only return when it gets “fair value” for its content, and Johnson flatly warned that the already record-long dispute could go on “for a little while.” He also insisted in a subsequent interview that “we’re ready to go for as long as they are,” implying that Disney was financially able to absorb whatever losses the dispute may produce.

From the perspective of reassuring the market, this was about the worst thing they could do. For many reasons, but at the end of the day, I’m just not sure Johnson’s bravado about being able to absorb blackout losses is supported by the numbers, and it looks like the market is beginning to agree with me. Even other publications have noted that Disney’s losses are around $4 million per day.

That estimate was meant to be a somber reflection of Disney’s high stakes in this battle, but I must say, that calculation seems to me to understate the damage that a permanent blackout of YouTube TV would do. That estimate implies $120 million per month, which, with YouTube TV at an estimated 10 million subscribers, pencils out to $12 per month, per subscriber.

The True Damage Of The Blackout

That seems impossibly low. In the first place, Disney is losing advertising impressions as well as subscription payments when it is blacked out. True, advertising contracts often preclude actual refunds; the standard structure of an ad contract with a major broadcaster like Disney is to set a contractually guaranteed minimum impressions level, which if not met results in “make-goods,” or free ad slots the next quarter or year to bring total impressions up to the guaranteed level.

So, even if we assume that Disney is not losing any advertising cash flows this year, the blackout will still make itself felt in subsequent quarters cash flows. Which means it is losing advertising income right now. Still, for now we’ll ignore this and assume the $12 refers solely to affiliate revenues.

This past year Disney reported $5.7 billion in Entertainment Linear Networks affiliate fees and an imputed $9.1 billion in Sports Linear fees after deducting ESPN+ subsidies. With Disney reporting its core linear networks, including ESPN and Disney Channel, have 61 million subscribers, that pencils out to $20.35 per month in fees, not $12. This number aligns much better with the $20 credits YouTube TV has issued subscribers for the blackout inconvenience; it is highly unlikely YouTube would overpay subscribers for the savings not paying ESPN is generating.

If YouTube TV were to permanently black out Disney’s networks, its affiliate revenue loss alone would come to almost $2.5 billion. Including advertising revenue, the total loss would come to $3.5 billion - at a time when the total operating income of the affiliate networks at the Sports and Entertainment divisions combined comes to only $3.4 billion.

Projecting An Endgame

My base scenario is still that this gets resolved; frankly, Disney cannot afford not to resolve it. The question is what such a resolution will cost. Almost certainly, what is driving Disney’s stance here is not ignorance of the rock, but awareness of the hard place.

As I’ve written about before, all of Disney’s deals with top distributors contain Most-Favored-Nation clauses; these are basically guarantees that if Disney offers improved economic terms to any of DIRECTV, Comcast (CMCSA) or Charter (CHTR) it will offer it to all of them. YouTube TV has already been offered a similar provision, so there is now basically a new Big Four in pay TV that is essentially a negotiating composite; whenever Disney bargains with one of them, it is in fact bargaining with all of them, since they all will receive any agreed concessions.

How Far Does The Rubble Fly?

The above calculations suggest that Disney’s rational concessions are capped at $3.4 billion for all four operators combined; that means that Disney can offer a maximum of $850 million in concessions to each one, assuming that they are all similar sizes over the lifetime of its new YouTube TV deal. Charter and Comcast are larger now, but YouTube TV is the only one still growing.

The concessions would all be offered on the affiliate fee side; that pencils out to about $7 per subscriber per month in reductions for the Big Four at the very, very most; obviously Disney would like to offer less than that. It might get away with less; YouTube TV is presumably suffering at least a little too, and it might well settle for less than a 35% reduction in its content costs.

As I said, it gets resolved; the question is the extent of the damage. If the full $3.4 billion comes out of Disney’s operating income, the post-tax hit comes to about $2.5 billion; that would knock its fair value price all the way down to $84. If the damage is only half, it’s $95.

Investment Summary

I believe the market is reacting to the growing perception that Disney has overplayed its hand with YouTube, not the aberration of what are almost certainly transitory declines in free cash flow. And that reaction is not unjustified; after treating the blackout dispute with unwarranted indifference, the market is now waking up to just how much of a threat an ESPN-free YouTube TV is.

While the decline is not warranted by the timing issues of Disney’s earnings, the risk to Disney is real, and I still rate it avoid even after the decline.

data.title: Strategy's Bitcoin Treasury: Different Securities For Different Investors (New Ratings)
data.description:
Once upon a time, there was a little business information software company called MicroStrategy.

It launched in 1989.

It went public in 1998, in time for the dot-com bubble and burst. Then, an accounting controversy in 2000 really sent it into a tailspin.

The SEC got into the act. Some restructuring followed. And the company limped along uneventfully—until 2011.

That’s when it adapted its software to the cloud. Then, in February 2020, it introduced a major new version of its offering.

Maybe the company would finally start to become a serious player in its market.

But alas, that was not to be.

It’s current market share remains mired at a measly 1.29%. (The leader, Tableau Software, has a 17.76% share.)

But that’s OK. Co-founder Michael Saylor had other, much bigger, ideas. It was to be about bitcoin.

The software business still exists. But the investment case for the company, having been renamed Strategy (NASDAQ: MSTR), is now tied to its…

Bold New Trails in Corporate Treasury

My initial 12/20/24 MSTR report quoted portions of the company’s 2021 10K (page 10) explaining its pivot to a new bitcoin-focused set of policies.

The corporate treasury would consist of the usual holdings of cash and cash equivalents.

But it would also include bitcoin. And this would be its “primary treasury reserve asset on an ongoing basis.”

In addition, the company adopted…

in conjunction with our Treasury Reserve Policy, a business strategy of purchasing bitcoin, and we may from time to time, subject to market conditions, issue debt or equity securities in capital raising transactions with the objective of using the proceeds to purchase bitcoin.

****

We view our bitcoin holdings as long-term holdings and we do not plan to engage in regular trading of bitcoin or to hedge or otherwise enter into derivative contracts with respect to our bitcoin holdings, though we may sell bitcoins in future periods as needed to generate cash for treasury management and other general corporate purposes.

To give you a sense of how serious this new vision is, take a look at how MSTR’s bitcoin treasury compares to treasuries of other major companies.

So here we are—goodbye software (not in fact, but as an investment theme). And hello, Bitcoin.

The Appeal of Bitcoin

At one time, I’d have classified anything related to bitcoin (or crypto) as scary. And I understand many still do.

My first reaction to bitcoin was to think of it as a collectible. How different is that from non-industrial demand for gold or silver? (And as I explained in my 12/20/24 writeup, there is a fixed maximum to the number of possible coins. After 21 million are issued, there will be no more. So, there’ll eventually be a built-in scarcity. That’s not so with precious metals.)

As a form of money, I understand how this can be a hard pill to swallow. Government-issued (fiat) currency is so deeply ingrained in our sensibilities, it’s hard to take anything else seriously.

But the definition of currency, or money, is and always has been much broader than that.

In my 5/9/25 MSTR writeup, I quoted “ Everyday Economics,” a booklet published by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, as having said:

Money is anything that is widely accepted as a form of payment for goods and services or repayment of debts.

In the limited economies of POW camps, cigarettes became money as soon as they became the accepted form of payment for rations that prisoners were exchanging.

(Emphasis supplied.)

Bitcoin clearly is part of “anything.” The only open question is “widely accepted.”

Today, its acceptance as a regular medium of exchange remains very limited. But the evolution of the blockchain (where coin ownership and transactions are verified and stored) is helping it gain ground. See generally, my 6/28/24 article on Coinbase (COIN).

And it’s gaining acceptance in the political, financial, and corporate communities.

Starting with politics, the Trump Administration brought a crypto-friendly climate to Washington.

This is a big deal.

Regulatory hostility has diminished and is turning more supportive. The July 2025 GENIUS Act boosts the legitimacy of stablecoins, an important link between crypto/blockchain and fiat currency. Again, see my 6/28/24 article on Coinbase for more on this.

Meanwhile, Wall Street is taking bitcoin more seriously.

So, too, are major banks.

And business community is beginning to embrace bitcoin.

Even Standard & Poor’s is adding an aura of legitimacy. It already added some crypto-friendly businesses to its flagship S&P 500 index.

MSTR isn’t included—yet. But it may happen in the future.

Bitcoin has had and will continue to have pricing ups and downs. But at this point, it’s hard to suggest it’ll become worthless, as early skeptics (once including me) feared.

Most likely, bitcoin will trade like a regular asset.

Consider…

Demand is rising due to its increasing legitimacy.

Meanwhile, supply will be limited. There’s a programmatic limit to the number of coins that can be created. After 21 million coins have been created, no new ones will ever be issued.

So despite periodic price slumps and corrections, I believe there will be a general upward bias.

An Extra Powerful Asset Play - Intelligent Leverage

Assuming, as I do, that bitcoin is a bona fide asset class, one might expect MSTR stock to be valued based on the worth of the company’s bitcoin holdings.

But there was always more to it than just that.

If one wants to simply play the ups and downs of bitcoin pricing, there are vehicles other than MSTR for that. There’s bitcoin itself. And for those who don’t want to deal with the mechanics of actually owning coins, there are ETFs that invest in it. See, for example, the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT).

MSTR’s goal is to have its stock (market cap) outperform bitcoin prices. It would do that through the manner in which it raises capital to finance its purchase of new coins. Choosing market conditions under which it issues new debt and/or equity would be one factor. The other would be the timing of bitcoin purchases.

This strategy has, so far, worked out magnificently. Even though bitcoin’s price has struggled recently, we see that percent changes in the value of MSTR’s bitcoin holdings dramatically outstripped percent changes in the company’s diluted shares and its market capitalization.

As tempting as it is to value MSTR stock based on the per-share value of the bitcoin it owns, the above chart confirms we should not thusly limit our computations.

Clearly, the company’s “intelligent leverage” is worth something.

Thinking this way would actually make for a fairly typical approach to equity valuation.

After all, how many stocks today trade at book value! Typically, companies trade at premiums to book based on expectations that management will do things to make it grow.

Similarly, it’s reasonable to assume that management’s use of intelligent leverage will enhance the worth of the company’s bitcoin position. So MSTR can legitimately trade at a premium to the current value of its bitcoin holdings.

The harder question is how much of a premium MSTR deserves.

Below is a historical summary of the stock’s premium.

With stocks, bonds, and real estate, there are theories and models we can use to assess, and argue about, valuation. Bitcoin is like precious metals in the sense that we don’t have objective valuation approaches.

With a bitcoin-based asset play like MSTR, the best we can do is note that the stock’s premium to asset value is now low relative to past highs. (It would be nice if it could get back to the discount we saw in 2022. But based on the market’s increased appreciation of intelligent leverage, a discount may no longer be in the cards.)

An Increasingly Active MSTR Treasury

Buying and holding bitcoin is half the story. The other half is getting capital to finance the purchases.

Capital raising at MSTR is much more compelling than at other companies.

The typical company can generate capital internally—through profit-making business operations and/or through income produced by non-operating assets.

MSTR has neither internal source. It must raise all of its capital externally.

Issuance of new common equity is an obvious part of the package. That’s the core of the intelligent leverage strategy discussed above.

Debt has been another part of the capital-raising strategy. Theoretically, this introduces a scary element. Debt comes with an interest-expense obligation.

But attaching an equity-conversion feature to the debt—as MSTR did when it borrowed—lets it dramatically slash its interest obligation. Most of MSTR’s convertible debt calls for interest at less than 1%. Some are zero-coupon issues. In these cases, borrowers expect to get all of their return through equity conversion.

But starting in January 2025, MSTR has been shifting gears. On the 10/31/25 analyst call, CEO Fong Lee discussed the company’s intent to allow its debt to vanish by 2029 through equity conversion. Instead, going forward, MSTR will rely on preferred equity financing. (Unlike with debt, skipping a preferred dividend does not constitute a default.)

So far this year, the company has completed a variety of preferred issues:

  • Strategy 8% Series A Perpetual Strike Preferred Stock (STRK)
  • Strategy 10% Series A Perpetual Strife Preferred Stock (STRF)
  • Strategy 9% Series A Perpetual Stretch Preferred Stock (STRC)
  • Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock (STRD)

Here’s a comparison of market characteristics.

If you haven’t guessed from looking at the table—and I wouldn’t blame you if you haven't—MSTR appears to offer one-stop shopping for a range of income-investor styles.

Think of this as a menu with dishes that appeal to different tastes.

Now, there are some things on the table that might seem odd.

For starters, you don’t typically think of preferred stock as having a duration.

That’s an important metric for fixed-income strategists, given how it measures bond volatility. But bonds have an important feature preferred shares lack—maturity dates. Those are critical to calculating duration.

But for preferred, there is a theoretical mathematical formulation that produces a duration for non-maturing preferred stock. It’s the Macaulay formula. This is (1+r)/r, where r is the yield or discount rate. That formula is easy to implement. But frankly, looking at its derivation gives me a migraine.

Asset coverage is another challenging area. Strictly speaking, all of MSTR’s bitcoin is unencumbered. But the table shows something MSTR calls BTC Rating. Slide 90 of MSTR’s latest earnings presentation defines this as…

the ratio of our Bitcoin NAV and the sum of the notional values of the instruments being rated and all instruments that are senior to and, if any liabilities share an equal claim to our assets, such instruments with a stated maturity date sooner than or that may become due upon an exercise of a repurchase right at the option of the holder sooner than, the liability being rated.

That’s a mouthful.

Essentially, the BTC Rating compares the value of the bitcoin MSTR owns to the value of the preferred issue. And in each case, the figure is adjusted for the fact that each preferred ranks differently in seniority. So higher ratings mean more bitcoin coverage of the preferred obligations.

As if this weren’t enough to contemplate, MSTR announced on 11/3/25 that it is issuing another 10% preferred issue, this one to be denominated in euros. Its ticker will be STRE.

And the company plans more overseas offerings.

This already makes for an intriguing set of investment choices. And the menu is slated to broaden.

But all this also presents…

Challenging Oddities and Important Risks

(1) Long-Term Financing to Cover Ordinary Expenses?

Back around 1975, while I was in law school, New York City was going through a major financial crisis. The professor in my Municipal Law class explained it as having been caused by the City’s having issued long-term bonds to cover ordinary operating expenses. Such bonds are supposed to be issued for major one-time projects, like bridges, highways, school construction, etc.

Taking those lessons to the corporate sector, we presume long-term debt or issuance of new equity should relate to some sort of one-time capital-type project, or acquisition, or something like that. Even with emerging companies or startups (e.g., quantum computing), we accept the issuance of permanent capital in order to fund expenses relating to building a new business.

But how comfortable would you be if an established company like Meta Platforms (META), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), or Microsoft (MSFT) were to issue long-term debt or new equity in order to raise money to meet its next regular monthly payroll expenses?

Speaking for myself, I’d panic.

Stretching the example, how would you feel if, say, Exxon Mobil (XOM) or Altria (MO) issued preferred stock to raise money so it could pay its next quarterly common dividend or the next set of interest payments due on its long-term debt?

Again, speaking for myself, I’d panic.

I’m not saying this is exactly what MSTR is doing…

Come to think of it, as I was deciding how to finish that previous sentence, I realized something. This is, indeed, exactly what MSTR is doing.

Cash preferred dividend payments are being funded by the issuance of preferred stock!

To be fair, MSTR is running a very different kind of business. And default risk here is minimal, more theoretical than real.

Here’s a tally of MSTR’s annual interest and preferred dividend obligations as of its latest earnings presentation.

Here is a summary of the company’s capital-raising activities

Clearly, the company has been able to raise a heck of a lot more money than it needs to meet its interest and dividend obligations. (New York City in 1975 couldn’t have even fantasized about a money-in/money-out relationship like this!)

And there is the unofficial asset backing. We saw in the valuation chart above that the market value of bitcoin held leaves interest and dividend obligations in the dust.

And it looks as if it should get easier to raise capital going forward. S&P recently assigned a B- rating to MSTR.

That’s high, but not the very top, in the “junk” range. (S&P prefers the word “speculative.”)

But as MSTR points out above, it is at least the first rating for a bitcoin treasury company.

Also, even B- gives MSTR access to a bigger capital pool than was the case when it was unrated.

(2) Bigger Plans

Recall above reference to the planned euro-denominated preferred issues. That’s just the first non-U.S. planned issuance.

Also, MSTR is working hard to expand awareness and distribution of its preferred issues.

This is good in the sense that it enhances the company’s ability to keep raising capital.

But it’s also concerning…

(3) Understandability

The harder MSTR works to issue and market preferred issues, the greater the probability that more of its preferred securities will wind up in accounts of people who don’t actually understand what all this is about.

As more people own securities they don’t understand, the more I worry.

I’ve seen the registration statements for the preferred issues. They’re fine.

Everything that needs to be disclosed is disclosed. For investors who aren’t inclined to read everything, the summaries in the early pages, while hardly literary, communicate well.

But I’ve been around in the investment field long enough to know what should happen often doesn’t. If things go wrong, I honestly cannot see any legal basis upon which disgruntled investors would be able to come after MSTR.

But the court of public opinion runs on different rules.

If things turn out such that enough people in the future wind up disappointed, MSTR and preferred securities are likely to suffer in price. And that would make it harder, or maybe even impossible, to execute the capital-raising portion of its business model.

(4) Expiration Date?

In pure logic, MSTR cannot persist perpetually.

There’s a finite number of potential bitcoins. The possible supply is programmatically capped at 21 million.

The world now has about 19.5 million coins.

It sounds like we’re close to the end. But reductions in the amount awarded over time are stretching the time when we’ll hit 21 million out into the future. (See generally, here.)

As of 10/26/25, MSTR owned 640,808 coins. That was 3.05% of the potential supply and 3.29% of the current supply.

So we can’t really say MSTR is cornering a fixed-supply market right now. But if we project far enough into the future, that might wind up happening.

That’s not a worry for today.

But as MSTR and others continue to accumulate bitcoin to hold in treasury, we have to wonder how far this can go before liquidity concerns arise and compromise the market. Many see NYSE/NASDAQ stocks as far more worth owning than less liquid OTC Pink Sheet stocks. Might bitcoin evolve toward the latter scenario?

But again, this is a theoretical, distant-future concern. It’s not a here-and-now worry.

What To Do About MSTR Stock - The Core Investment Vehicle

MSTR shares have been weak since my initial 12/20/24 writeup, when I said, “Hold.” Since then, the stock is down 37%, versus a 16% gain for the S&P 500.

I upgraded to “Buy” on 5/8/25 given my increased understanding of bitcoin. But since then, the stock is down 44%, versus a 21% gain for the S&P 500.

Obviously, those ratings aged poorly.

During this time, Bitcoin has cooled off.

Another factor in MSTR’s poor performance here has been the narrowing of the stock’s premium to the value of its bitcoin holdings.

Also, I suspect a good deal of buying interest in the preferred shares might have absorbed funds that otherwise might have been invested in MSTR had the latter been less aggressive in creating a variety of income offerings.

Finally, this situation has become a lot more complex than it was when I reviewed it in the past. Complexity does not help share prices.

Here’s the MSTR share price.

This is a bad chart.

I hate how the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) is below and falling faster than the also weak 50-day EMA.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) give no solace. Both measure which party to trade is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both indicators look terrible right now.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding, or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong…” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

For now, I see MSTR as likely performing no better than the broader market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating MSTR as a “Hold.”

What To Do About the Preferred Issues - The Satellite Investment Vehicles

Right now, I’m much more interested in the preferred issues.

Maybe it’s my age. The idea of a good-yielding bitcoin play appeals to me.

I get that there’s an eventual theoretical date as of which MSTR’s business model has to expire. And I don’t love the idea of permanent capital being issued to cover ongoing interest and dividend expenses.

S&P has it right—This is a “junk bond” type credit.

But I’m a former junk bond fund manager. (That was back in the mid-1980s heyday, when Michael Milken and Drexel Burnham ruled the field.) I know how to live with credits rated this way, and worse.

I’ve seen a lot of bad things in the junk arena. And I’ve invested in things with coverages (interest back in the day or preferred dividends today) that were much more challenging than what we see here. And I’ve accepted those despite much lesser yields.

For your convenience, here again, is the table I showed above summarizing the preferred issues.

As to STRK

Here’s a 1-year STRK-MSTR share price performance comparison.

STRK appears to be doing exactly what it’s supposed to do. It’s a good income vehicle with exposure to the core MSTR bitcoin treasury business.

But its yield mutes some of the volatility. We really see that in the most recent price action.

Other preferred issues are more stable. But the combination of yield and convertibility works for me.

Given my belief in an upward bias for bitcoin prices, convertibility, the good yield, and the reduced volatility relative to MSTR, I rate STRK as a “Buy.”

As to STRC, STRF, and STRD

Here’s a 3-month share price comparison between MSTR and STRK, and the newer STRC, STRF, and STRD issues.

I’m giving a thumbs down, on STRD.

In terms of price performance, it’s similar to STRF. Meanwhile, STRD has a higher yield.

But STRD is a non-cumulative preferred. That means that if the company chooses to skip a dividend payment, it’s gone forever.

STRF, on the other hand, is a cumulative preferred (as are the other preferred issues). That means if the company skips a dividend payment, it has to pay all skipped-and-in-arrears preferred dividends before it can resume paying in the future.

To my thinking, that extra protection is worth a lesser yield.

So I rate STRD “Sell” (meaning I expect it to underperform).

Right now, STRC looks like a great income vehicle—obviously, for those who can live with the above-described risks.

It has the lowest volatility. And it has a terrific yield.

But that adjustability feature (see footnote in the above table) bears watching. It sounds great now. If the stock falls, MSTR will quickly adjust and raise the payout to drive the price back up toward $100.

That said, we have to always keep an eye on this issue. If, in the future, bitcoin rallies and causes STRC to rise, we have to expect a dividend reduction. Remember—management will continue to target a $100 share price.

Still, for now, STRC looks poised to outperform. I express that as a “Buy” rating for the stock.

Meanwhile, STRF is what I’d consider to be the most basic issue.

Its combination of yield, volatility, and BTC Rating looks well balanced. And in contrast to STRC, the dividend for STRF will be stable—and in contrast to STRD, cumulative.

So I see STRF as an outperformer. Accordingly, I rate it as a “Buy.”

data.title: Applied Materials Q4: The Fundamentals Slow Down, But The Stock Doesn't
data.description:
Introduction

Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT) is one of the most interesting players in the semiconductor sector because there aren’t many alternatives in its niche (at least not for U.S. investors). On the American market, you basically have Applied Materials and Lam Research (LRCX). Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY) is also a major competitor, but it’s a Japanese company, and as we know, U.S. investors don’t love ADRs. So, from a practical investing standpoint, the “competition” is really AMAT and LAM, at least for the attention of the U.S. investors.

It’s a very interesting player because AMAT’s business is extremely specialized, although crucial for the semiconductor industry. The company handles the processes that build the layers of a semiconductor. They take the silicon wafer (which is basically a silicone plate that looks like a wafer), and they use different materials in solid or gas form, vaporize them, and then let them settle and attach to the wafer’s surface. That’s how they create transistors, interconnects, and all the microchips that are used to create chips later on (I am not an engineer, so I explain it as well as my understanding allows me).

The company is not doing badly, but it’s not perfect either. They just closed their sixth consecutive year of growth, despite trade restrictions and a very unfavorable market mix. They pay a small dividend, they remain profitable, and the valuation looks almost cheap, which is not something we see in semiconductor-related stocks.

At the same time, the stock is down 5% pre-market after the earnings, and I am very cautious about it as well.

I broke down Applied Materials back in mid-August and gave the stock a Hold rating. The stock has done really well since then, but I am afraid the surge was fueled by the AI sentiment, and not the fundamentals of AMAT. Looking back, I still think the Hold rating was the right move. And today, even after the latest earnings, and even after the market pullback, I still think the stock is overvalued.

Quarterly Results

Now, looking at the latest results, the company closed its sixth consecutive year of growth. Revenue is at an all-time high, gross margin is at a 25-year high, EPS is also at a record, and the company continues paying a small dividend and buying back shares. If you look only at the headline numbers, you might think they are great, and be curious why the stock is falling. But when you go into more detail, the picture becomes more nuanced.

First of all, Q4 itself was not a strong quarter, and this wasn’t a surprise because management told us in Q3 that Q4 would be softer. They were actually very open about it, explaining that the weakening primarily comes from reduced demand in China and the impact of the trade restrictions. Revenue fell about three percent year over year, operating income fell around six percent, and EPS also fell about six percent. Gross margin stayed high, but this is because they sold a richer mix of advanced systems and also raised prices, so it wasn’t purely an organic improvement. If you focus on the quarterly trend instead of the annual one, you can clearly see that the momentum got weaker, and that’s not something we’ve seen from Applied Materials in recent years, because for six years everything was steadily climbing.

Speaking of the segment breakdown, Semiconductor Systems, which is their biggest and most important business line, dropped from around thirty-six percent non-GAAP operating margin to thirty-two percent. Global Services stayed stable at around twenty-eight percent, which makes sense because services and spare parts tend to be more recurring and less cyclical. But semiconductor systems are the main line, and this is where the growth should be coming from, ideally.

China & Overall growth

China used to be one of the biggest markets for semiconductor equipment, roughly forty percent of global wafer fab equipment spending. But because of the U.S. restrictions, Applied Materials cannot supply the same range of tools anymore, and Chinese fabs are actively increasing the use of domestic alternatives. In Q4, China represented around twenty-five percent of Applied Materials’ revenues, down from much higher levels in the past, and management openly said that in 2026, China will be even lower. They also said they are not assuming further restrictions, which I agree with, but even without new restrictions, the direction is still downward because the current limitations already reduce what China can buy (this and all of the following quotes are taken from the earnings call transcript):

China declined to 28% of our total systems and service revenues in fiscal 2025 and to 25% for our fourth quarter. In 2026, we expect wafer fab equipment spending in China to be lower, and we are not anticipating significant changes to market restrictions.

This wouldn’t be such a big problem if the company was growing fast in other regions, but that’s not really what we’re seeing. They did hit record revenue in Taiwan and Korea, which is good, but globally, the growth is still in the very high single digits. And this brings me to the part of the earnings call that I personally did not love at all. In the press release and on the call, they say they’re “preparing operations and service organization to support higher demand beginning in the second half of calendar 2026,” and they explicitly say this is based on customer conversations (see the last bullet in the Earnings Presentation Screenshot 1 above).

For me, this is a very different thing from having signed contracts or orders in the backlog. When a company already has agreements in place and just hasn’t delivered yet, you can see it in accounts receivable, you can see it in the order book, and you at least have something concrete behind all the optimism. Here, it sounds more like, “we visited Asia, talked to customers, they told us the opportunities have never been greater and they’ll need more tools,” which is nice to hear, but not exactly a number to support it with. In the same paragraph, management points to third-party forecasts that expect the semiconductor industry to grow 10–15% annually over the next five years, and I don’t disagree with that. But if you go back to my earlier breakdown of the semiconductor sector, you’ll see that the last five years were actually much stronger for semiconductors than the next five are likely to be. The problem is that even during that boom, AMAT grew noticeably slower than the overall industry. So it’s not that I think growth disappears; I just don’t see enough evidence that Applied Materials will suddenly capture a much larger part of it than they did before.

I recently returned from an extended trip to Asia. My discussions with customers and partners reinforce my view that opportunities for the semiconductor industry and Applied Materials have never been greater. Our customers are engaging with us to ensure we are ready to support significant production ramps in the coming years. AI has reached a tipping point that is accelerating investment in next-generation computing infrastructure and advanced silicon. Today, we are seeing a virtuous cycle of innovation and demand. Advances in performance, energy consumption and cost of AI computing open up new AI applications that in turn, significantly increase demand for AI compute capacity. Recent third-party forecasts predict that the semiconductor industry will grow at a compound annual rate between 10% to 15% over the next 5 years, driving a healthy increase in wafer fab equipment spending. We expect 2026 to be another growth year for Applied with our revenue being weighted toward the second half of the calendar year.

To be more concrete, if we look at the last five years, Applied Materials' revenue grew by around fifty-seven percent in total. Let’s compare it to the different players of the industry - a fab, a fabless designer, and even a memory manufacturer. TSMC (TSM) grew by one hundred fifty-four percent. AMD grew by two hundred twenty-eight percent. Micron (MU), which is extremely cyclical and had its own downturns, grew sixty-nine percent. The strongest semiconductor boom of our lifetime happened, and Applied Materials grew the slowest among these. So when management says they expect strong growth in the next few years while third-party estimates project ten to fifteen percent annual growth for the industry, I have a really hard time believing Applied Materials will suddenly start outperforming when they have historically underperformed during one of the best periods for semiconductors ever.

​​Valuation

In my opinion, this company is not as cheap as it appears to be, and the fundamentals do not match the stock’s behavior at all.

If we look only at absolute numbers, we could get a feeling that everything looks natural and right. In the chart below, the stock price, gross profit margin, and revenue all grow together, so there is no disconnect.

But then, if we look at the normalized chart (with the percentage change), we see that gross profit margin grew by about five percent, revenue grew by roughly nine percent, and the stock price grew by around one hundred percent. That’s why I think the stock has outpaced the fundamentals.

Even if we look at Seeking Alpha's valuation metrics, which I personally think are very useful when comparing expected growth to actual growth, Applied Materials looks overvalued across multiple metrics. The PEG ratio is too high for this level of growth, the price-to-sales and EV-to-sales both show the company trading at a large premium to the sector median, and the forward-looking growth grades for revenue, EBITDA, and EPS are simply not strong enough to justify the stock’s current valuation.

So I honestly think the current decline makes much more sense than the rally we saw earlier this year. The rally was entirely driven by AI enthusiasm and the idea that semiconductor equipment companies will automatically benefit from every expansion in AI infrastructure. And while it is true that equipment spending will grow, because you cannot build AI chips without it, it doesn’t automatically mean that every company in this segment will grow equally well. Historically, Applied Materials captured much less of the upside compared to the industry leaders, and this is true to this very day.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I continue to see Applied Materials as a very solid and very respectable business, but not as a stock that justifies its current valuation. They maintain strong margins, and they remain consistently profitable, but the stock price simply got too far ahead of the fundamentals during the AI rally. And even though the recent pullback looks meaningful, in my opinion, the shares are still expensive for what the company is currently delivering. I would personally feel much more comfortable with this stock somewhere around the one-hundred-fifty-dollar level, where the valuation would match both the growth rate and the risk profile. Until then, it remains a hold for me.

data.title: Why Berkshire Hathaway Remains A Core Buy Going In To 2026
data.description:
Berkshire is still one of my core holdings

It's hard to deny that this market is expensive; regardless of that sentiment, investors continue to put a buoy under the broader indexes and continue to drive them higher. To me, there's logic to it. AI is revolutionary, is useful, and is already providing tangible ROI to those that know how to wield it. The recent round of layoffs by Amazon (AMZN) and others in tech is a pretty telling sign.

I am of the mindset that I know a good deal when I see one, and I only buy stuff that I want to hold on to regardless of the volatility that ensues thereafter; thus, price is of optimal importance to me. Right now, when I don't see a whole lot of prices I like, at least not to be able to buy with conviction, this is when I default to my core conservative sleeve. This has evolved over time, but Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)(BRK.A) and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) are my default buys when rebalancing some gains or simply when the overall market is too expensive.

I will toggle back to growth stocks, the S&P500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq (QQQ) in drawdowns. Right now is certainly one of those Berkshire Hathaway moments for me.

A great Q3 from Berkshire

When evaluating Berkshire Hathaway earnings, one has to be somewhat of an artist rather than a graphic design specialist. You need to be one that has read the letters and understands how Berkshire operates. Earnings can be skewed in one direction or another by trading activity, which is not the central theme of what makes Berkshire tick. Ultimately they want to buy and hold as many things in their publicly listed stock portfolio segments rather than sell and pay taxes. Therefore, the first thing I like to do is back out the investment gains/losses line and just focus in on the core privately held business interests of the company.

These are :

  • Insurance-underwriting
  • Insurance-investment income
  • BNSF
  • Berkshire Hathaway Energy
  • Manufacturing, service, and retailing [the other businesses category]

I basically envision Berkshire as a pie with three slices. The famed holdings portfolio consists of stocks. The cash and equivalents segment and the privately held businesses. At this point, the Berkshire stock holdings are a massive list of blue chips that do not deviate too far from what the market does under the surface, if we looked at the weighted beta, I would bet the correlation is fairly strong. Berkshire can also not control interest rates on their cash, those will produce the amount of interest income that the short end of the curve dictates in any given year. They can control the private businesses and also how much they keep in cash reserves to pursue new deals.

Let's take a look at how the core operating businesses did in Q3.

All numbers in millions of USD, data from Berkshire Q3 report:

Segment same quarter comparison yoy 2024 2025 % Growth
Insurance – Underwriting 750 2,369 216%
Insurance – Investment Income 3,664 3,181 -13%
BNSF 1,383 1,449 4.8%
Berkshire Hathaway Energy 1,629 1,489 -8.6%
Manufacturing/Service/Retail 3,342 3,616 8.2%
Other -678 1,381
Metric 2024 2025 % Change
Total Operating Earnings (ex-investment gains/losses) 10,090 13,485 +33.7%

These were some solid year-over-year improvements, and it was a relief to see insurance underwriting get back to normal. This segment hit Berkshire Hathaway hard due to the LA wildfires previously. The manufacturing, service, and retail items did much better year over year. The "other" category is largely comprised of accounting markups and markdowns as well as FX, from my understanding, also swinging to the positive in this quarter versus the same last year. Overall, a nice positive increase in the operating business year over year for the same quarter.

The balance sheet

We're now going to look at the three components of Berkshire, the cash and equivalents, the equity portfolio, and the operating businesses—to assess where a theoretical price-to-earnings ratio lies based on the income each segment produces on a forward basis.

Firstly, the cash segment:

Now with cash increasing to a total of $381.673 billion, I will use the yield of The State Street® SPDR® Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) as a proxy for cash equivalent rates. This currently yields 4.2% and is most likely headed to below 4% in the 12 months ahead. I will give the cash a conservative yield of 3.9%. This would produce $14.885 billion in interest income for the company for a full 12 months.

Next we get to the holdings segment:

Their last reported 13F filing for Q2 2025 included $257,521,776,925

While there is some difference between the most recent 10-Q and the most recent 13-F, I will use the data of the 13 F to value the holdings portfolio.

Lastly, we get the remainder of our value, representative of the operating businesses, by subtracting the holdings + the cash equivalents from the market cap, being the price for which we as investors can acquire these assets on the open market:

Thus, at $1,100,000,000,000 [market cap] -($257,521,776,925+$381,673,000,000) = $460,805,223,075 for the operating companies. So the Berkshire pie looks like this as of current:

Now we need to invert and figure out the earnings yield of each segment.

Cash is easy; using 3.9%, we just divide 1 by .039 to get an equivalent P/E of 25.64. The weight in the market cap is 34.7%; thus, 34.7% X 25.64 = 8.89

The operating businesses ex-trading activity produced a net income in the current quarter of $13,485 million [$13.485 billion]. If we project a conservative 2% growth rate per quarter for the next 4 quarters, we would project operating income of $55,579 million as a forward income projection for this segment [$55.579 billion]. With the operating business valued at $460.8 billion in this thesis, that would make the P/E of this segment $460.8 billion/$55.579 billion = 8.29X. Multiply this by 41.9% = 3.4738.

Finally, take a look through the earnings of the portfolio, or the average weighted P/E ratio of the holdings company stocks that Berkshire holds.

Ticker Company Name Portfolio Weight (%) TTM P/E Ratio Weight × P/E
AAPL Apple Inc. 50.08% 27.94X 14.00
BAC Bank of America Corp. 8.28% 11.08X 0.92
AXP American Express Co. 7.63% 18.33X 1.40
KO Coca-Cola Co. 7.15% 24.17X 1.73
CVX Chevron Corp. 6.70% 21.47X 1.44
OXY Occidental Petroleum Corp. 4.19% 30.17X 1.26
KHC Kraft Heinz Co. 3.53% 14.36X 0.51
MCO Moody’s Corp. 2.68% 42.88X 1.15
CB Chubb Ltd. 1.83% 15.53X 0.28
V Visa Inc. 0.90% 27.52X 0.25

This constitutes 92.97% of the portfolio with a weighted average of a 22.94X P/E ratio. We'll conservatively bump this up to an even 23X inclusive of the rest of the stocks in the holdings.

Therefore, 23 X 23.4% = 5.382.

Add up all the weighted P/E ratios, and I come to an estimate of [8.89 + 3.4738 + 5.382]= 17.74X weighted average P/E. This has fallen a bit from my prior analysis of 18.4X through a combination of sideways to down share price action and improvements in earnings. With a forward market P/E ratio of about 23X, Berkshire trades at a conservative discount to market valuations in my opinion, with a "Berkshire put" in place if the stock gets down to 1.25X book value that Berkshire owners hope remains in place under new leadership.

This is the assumption that the cash pile would be used to buy back shares should they ever hit a price-to-book ratio of 1.25X. This would represent a 21.1% price decline from here and a possible floor around $404/share for the B shares.

May 2026 will be the first year without Warren Buffett leading the annual meeting

Next year it will be very interesting to see what type of turnout the annual Berkshire meeting generates. He may make a cameo appearance, but both the annual letter and the primary speaking duties are squarely in the hands of new CEO Greg Abel.

More Japanese buys in bound?

Berkshire also initiated another exploration of yen-denominated bond offerings. The company has purchased significant stakes in the 5 largest Japanese trading houses, which have all done very well to date:

I have covered these companies extensively in previous articles and have also developed an equal-weight copycat portfolio of this segment on my own. These are some of the best total return plays in Japanese stock market history that have returned alpha to investors versus the broader US market indexes regardless of the poor performance of the Japanese stock market over the last decade. These remain cheap, and Berkshire remains poised to continue adding.

Risks and summary

The annual meeting and letter will be scrutinized heavily; negative headlines could hit the stock regardless of the underlying company performance. For those that study Berkshire and what they have built over the years, there are very few things that can sway us from our opinion on the company and why it deserves a spot as a conservative core portfolio holding along with your broader market index funds. The company has now hoarded a ton of cash, and that puts it in a de-risked position versus other diversified mutual funds and ETF plays that do not have a large cash component, in my opinion.

The cash return will, of course, lag what they could get if deployed, but also represents opportunity in a crisis. It also represents a fortress of solitude that has a doomsday anti-failure mechanism built in. I like to think of it as padlocked iron shorts in case the tide goes out. Berkshire remains a buy, and I look forward to the full transition to Abel's leadership in 2026. Buy.

data.title: Diamond Hill Select Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets continued their year-to-date rise in Q3, with US stocks gaining over 8% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index) and taking calendar-year gains to over 14%. Small-cap stocks had the best quarter, up over 12%, while large caps gained roughly 8% and mid caps were up 5%. From a style perspective, growth led at the larger end of the cap spectrum, while small-cap growth and value stocks delivered nearly identical returns—with small value slightly ahead (up over 12%).

From a sector perspective, the artificial intelligence (AI)-fueled boom in technology (13%) and communication services (12%) stocks continues, with the two sectors leading and driving much of the index’s positive return. Another meaningful positive contributor was the consumer discretionary sector, up nearly 9%. The sole sector in the red in Q3 was consumer staples, which fell -2.5%.

3Q25 Russell 3000 Index Sector Returns (%)

Trade, monetary policy and geopolitics remained the top headlines in Q3, even as markets seemed to shrug off any ongoing concerns and continue their rise (and rebound from late Q1’s and early Q2’s sharp downturn in the wake of President Trump’s initial tariff announcements). Despite the uncertainty stemming from these and other areas, markets plodded along in Q3, with several indices notching new all-time highs. Technology companies have continued their torrid run as sentiment around the potential for AI to radically change life as we know it remains high.

Against such a backdrop, it can be challenging to fight the urge to float with the current—but we believe investors who do the hard work of researching and identifying high-quality companies the markets may be entirely overlooking can ultimately generate attractive long-term returns. As such, we will continue focusing on areas where we believe valuations are disconnected from underlying fundamentals and long-term growth outlooks— an approach we believe will benefit investors regardless of how events continue unfolding in the quarters and years ahead.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio trailed the Russell 3000 Index in Q3. Relative weakness was partially attributed to our information technology exposure—both our underweight to the index’s top-performing sector and our holdings, which, though positive, trailed benchmark peers. Likewise, our consumer discretionary holdings also posed a relative headwind. Conversely, our industrials and financials holdings were sources of relative strength.
